planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 55°F; Bloody Butcher is a 150+-year-old Appalachian and Virginia heirloom dent corn with dramatic deep blood-red and red-flecked kernels, used for meal, hominy, roasting ears, and heritage bourbon production — and one of the most visually striking of all heirloom corns.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks of at least 4 rows to ensure cross-pollination; plant in fertile, well-amended soil with consistent moisture during pollination and kernel fill. Bloody Butcher is a tall, vigorous Appalachian heirloom dent corn documented in Virginia and the Appalachian region since at least the 1840s; the plants are impressive, reaching 10–12 feet, and produce 2–4 ears per stalk of medium-length (10–14 inch) ears with kernels that are dramatic deep blood-red to red-brick with darker red cob — the most vividly colored of the traditional red dent corns. Dried, the kernels are ground into deeply flavored, reddish-tinged cornmeal with a rich, earthy, sweet corn taste that is distinct from modern yellow dent meal. Also used for hominy, grits, masa, and heritage whiskey distilling. At the milk stage (80–90 days) the ears can be eaten as roasting ears; for grain and meal, allow to dry on the stalk or cure indoors.

planting.note: Direct sow at last frost — Painted Mountain tolerates light frost and can be planted earlier than most corns; it was developed in Montana by Dave Christensen specifically for cold, short-season mountain growing conditions, maturing in 65–85 days from seed at altitudes over 5,000 feet.

directions: Sow in blocks of at least 4 rows after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Painted Mountain is shorter than most corns and somewhat drought-tolerant once established. Painted Mountain corn was developed over 30 years by Dave Christensen of Montana through selection from hundreds of traditional Native American and pioneer corn varieties, specifically to produce an open-pollinated flour corn that matures in 65–85 days in the short growing seasons of Montana mountain communities; the resulting variety is extremely early-maturing, cold-tolerant, and drought-resistant compared to most corns. The ears are 6–8 inches long with strikingly beautiful, multi-colored kernels in a palette of crimson, blue, gold, yellow, orange, and white that can produce rainbow-like color gradients even on a single ear. The flour ground from Painted Mountain has sweet, complex flavor; the variety is also grown as a ornamental corn and for whole-grain cooking.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil in blocks; Oaxacan Green is a pre-Columbian green-kerneled dent corn from the Oaxaca Valley of Mexico, grown for thousands of years as a staple flour corn and still used to make green-tinged tortillas and tlayudas with an earthy, rich, grassy flavor profile distinct from yellow or white corns.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks of 4+ rows after last frost; Oaxacan Green is a tall, productive corn that needs ample space and fertile soil — plant in isolation from other corn varieties to maintain the distinctive green kernel color through seed saving. Oaxacan Green is an ancient pre-Columbian dent corn of the Zapotec peoples of Oaxaca, Mexico, one of the most visually distinctive of all heritage corns: the kernels are a translucent jade-to-emerald green when dried, unlike any other corn color, and produce a greenish-tinged masa with a distinctive earthy, herbal, rich flavor when ground. Used for green-tinged tortillas, tlayudas (the large flat Oaxacan tortillas), and whole-grain tamales in traditional Oaxacan cooking. The tall (7–11 feet), vigorous plants also produce striking ornamental ears with luminous green kernels contrasting against the red cob. Seed Savers Exchange lists this as one of the most unique and irreplaceable heritage corns in North America.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil in blocks of 4+ rows; Pencil Cob is a distinctive North American heritage corn with narrow, pencil-thin cobs densely packed with small, cream-to-yellow kernels — used primarily as a flour corn for fine-grained, delicate-flavored cornmeal and grits prized in the Appalachian and mid-Atlantic tradition.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in blocks of 4+ rows for good pollination; thin to 9–12 inches within the row. Pencil Cob corn (sometimes called Cob Corn or Ashworth type) is a distinctive Appalachian and Midwestern heritage flour corn named for the distinctive narrow, slender cob (roughly pencil-diameter, compared to the fist-thick cobs of modern hybrids) that is densely covered in small, tightly packed kernels; the cobs are long for their diameter (8–12 inches) and produce flour corn with a fine-grained texture and delicate, sweet corn flavor that is different from the coarser, more assertive flavor of modern hybrid cornmeal. Ground into a fine, cream-colored meal that makes exceptionally tender and flavorful cornbread; many Southern cornbread enthusiasts claim it produces the finest cornbread of any heritage variety. Also used for hominy, grits, and polenta.

common_name: Eight-Row Flint Corn (New England Flint, Rhode Island Flint)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in blocks; Eight-Row Flint is the original New England corn of the Narragansett, Wampanoag, and other Indigenous peoples of the Northeast — the corn that the Pilgrims learned to grow and which sustained early colonial New England, and the genetic ancestor of nearly all modern flour and sweet corn varieties.

directions: Sow in blocks of 4+ rows after last frost; Eight-Row Flint is earlier-maturing than most heritage corns and well-suited to shorter northern seasons. Eight-Row Flint (also called New England Flint, Rhode Island Flint, and Narragansett Flint) is the traditional corn of the Algonquian peoples of the northeastern United States — the corn that sustained the Wampanoag, Narragansett, Mohegan, and neighboring peoples for centuries before European contact, and the corn that taught early colonists of Plymouth and Massachusetts Bay how to survive in New England. It bears exactly eight rows of hard, flinty, small kernels in orange-red, white, or multi-colored patterns on slender 7–9 inch ears; the flint kernel type (hard, glass-like outer layer rather than dented) grinds into a fine, sweet, dense cornmeal. This is the corn of traditional New England johnnycakes, the grain type tested by Narragansett Flint Corn restoration projects. A critical piece of American agricultural heritage.
